topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across my screen, a stark, unwelcome rectangle of pixels. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that burned in my chest. I stared at the listing, the familiar peeling paint, the overgrown garden I’d spent a decade trying to tame, all suddenly packaged for transaction. A tremor started in my hands, making the phone clatter onto the worn wooden floor. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy, unrecognizable. I slammed my laptop shut, the resulting thud echoing in the sudden silence of the apartment. I paced the small living room, my steps quick and jerky, running a hand repeatedly through my already disheveled hair. Every corner of the room seemed to shrink, pressing in on me. I needed to check. I needed to see if it was a trick, a cruel joke played by some bored ghost.

[story 2]

I read the details three times, my eyes scanning the property description as if searching for a hidden flaw, a typo that would invalidate the entire existence of the listing. My jaw tightened until my molars ached. It was impossible. This place was etched into my memory, every crooked window frame, every scent of dust and old cedar. Now it was just a commodity, a number on a spreadsheet. I stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I pressed my palms hard against my eyes, trying to physically block the image of that house from my vision. "This isn't happening," I muttered, my voice tight with suppressed agitation. I began pacing the length of my kitchen counter, picking up and dropping ceramic mugs with unnecessary force, the clatter a frantic punctuation mark in the quiet room.

[story 3]

My fingers hovered over the 'contact agent' button, trembling so violently I had to steady them with my other hand. The sheer audacity of it—someone else wanting *my* history, *my* sanctuary—sent a wave of intense frustration washing over me. I didn't click anything. Instead, I threw the tablet onto the sofa cushion, the impact jarring. I stood rigid for a long moment, my chest heaving with rapid, shallow breaths. I walked over to the window again, not looking out at the street, but staring at the reflection of my own strained face superimposed over the listing photo. A low growl rumbled in my throat, a sound born of profound, contained fury. I turned sharply, stalking toward the bedroom, needing to be somewhere quiet, somewhere where the ghost of that old house couldn't follow.
